column1: # THE LONG NIGHT — The Crossroads (meta-progression, design.md §13)
# Owner: Jane. Machine-readable. John: parse this; don't invent gold costs.
# Tab-separated. '#' comments and blank lines ignored.
# Gold persists between runs. It drops from chests, elites, and 1-in-40 ordinary
# kills. You spend it here, between runs, and it never comes back.
# Without these the cost curve below is a number with no denominator. Measured,
#   winning run       1,365g   ->  11 runs to buy everything
# The 11-vs-35 spread is the point: winning is the fast path, so the meta rewards
# getting good rather than grinding losses. The Beggar collapsing it to 5 is also
# the point -- "weak damage, rich runs" is his entire identity, and he costs 900g,
# so you can only choose that shortcut after the meta has already begun.

column4: fraction of ordinary kills that drop gold
only if you actually kill her

column1: # cost(level) = cost_base * cost_growth^(level-1), rounded to the nearest 10.
# levels = how many times it can be bought. kind: mult | add | unlock.
# stat names match passives.tsv where they overlap, so John reuses one applier.
# THE RULE: meta-progression may make a bad run survivable. It may never make a
# good run trivial. Nothing here touches weapon damage scaling or the spawn
# curve; it only moves the floor, never the ceiling. A player who has bought
# everything should still lose to the Countess if they build badly.

column10: remove a card from the pool for the run
come back once at 50% HP. expensive on purpose.

column1: # --- characters. one-off unlocks; see characters.tsv for their stats ---------
# --- endless mode. unlocked by seeing dawn, not by gold ----------------------

column10: FREE, but locked until you first survive the night. the sun never rises; at 30:00 the Reapers come and cannot be killed.
